For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Jun-27-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Hiren S. Bakrania, Esq., CLAUSEN MILLER P.C. (949) 260-3100 hbakrania@clausen.com Attorneys for Defendant MERCURY ENGINEERING GROUP, INC
No other appearances

Not contested. Not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ROSEMARIE MACGUINNESS, PHILIP BELLBER's Determination Of Good Faith Settlement.

Plaintiffs' motion for determination of good faith settlement with defendants DAWSON-CLINTON GENERAL CONTRACTORS CORP. and SF GARAGE COMPANY, INC. is denied without prejudice.

While the contesting party has the burden of proof on this motion, substantial evidence must still be present for the court to grant it. (CCP 877.6(d) ["The party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue."]; Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1350, n.6. [settlors must provide substantial evidence to support a critical assumption as to the nature and extent of its liability].)
When determining whether a settlement was entered into in good faith the court should take into account a rough approximation of plaintiff's total recovery and defendants' proportionate liability, the amount paid in settlement, and recognize that a settlor should pay less in settlement than if he were found liable after trial. The court should also consider the financial condition of the settlor, the existence of insurance, and any evidence of collusion. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assoc. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.)

In this case, plaintiffs rely on an attorney declaration from Samuel Ray and nothing more. That declaration explains that plaintiffs contend that their damages are $1,645,767.49 while settling defendants believe the amount is closer to $350,585. There is no information regarding the settling defendants' precise roles on the project or their proportionate fault. The court is only told that Dawson-Clinton was the general contractor and SF Garage was hired "to perform work" on the property. (Ray Decl., par. 10.) While the proposed settlement sums are large, competent evidence is necessary for the court to assess the settling defendants' proportionate liability to grant this motion.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-18-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MERCURY ENGINEERING GROUP, INC.'s DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's "demurrer" filing has several defects and thus is overruled without prejudice to filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings.

No demurrer was filed. A demurrer is a free-standing document, captioned as such, that must distinctly and separately specify its grounds by paragraph. (CCP 430.60; CRC 3:1320; Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 7:99-110.)

Even had a demurrer been filed, defendant is noncompliant with CCP 430.41. Defendant's 430.41 declaration states: "This form must be filed with the demurrer," but the form declaration was filed five days after the "demurrer" papers. Further, the declaration was attached to a "notice of errata," but an errata notice corrects mistakes in a document's contents, it does not cure late filings. Moreover, defendant's check-box form does not reflect current CCP 430.41 law. (See, e.g., id. at (a)(1) ["or by video conference"].)

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

MINI MINUTES: No appearances, tentative ruling adopted as follows: DEFENDANT MERCURY ENGINEERING GROUP, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is moot. On May 2, 2022 Plaintiff filed a Notice of Settlement. On May 10, 2022 Plaintiff presented an unopposed ex-parte application for DOE Amendment Only (Ex-parte 1:25-26) re-asserting that a settlement was reached with all other parties (Ex-parte 2:4). On May 12, 2022 the Court issued an order granting leave to amend the complaint limited to the DOE amendment. The Court was not asked to and did not rule on a continued litigation against parties in the Notice of Settlement. The Court notes that Mercury Engineering is not named as a Doe Defendant in the First Amended Complaint and the Court could not locate any mention on Mercury Engineering in the body of the First Amended Complaint. The Court further notes that Plaintiff changed the caption of the case without leave of Court. Therefore, the First Amended Complaint in its present form, filed without leave of Court is stricken. Plaintiff shall have until December 9, 2022 to either (1) file an amended complaint clearly identifying Mercury Engineering as a DOE defendant and being the sole defendant in this case; or (2) to file a motion for leave to file an amended complaint re-naming the existing defendants (in light of the Notice of Settlement) and naming Mercury Engineering as a Doe. Prevailing party to prepare order in compliance with CRC 3.1312(b). Judge: Ronald E. Quidachay; Clerk: J. Murphy; Not Reported. (D501)
